item.title: Protester says they are willing to close Ireland down over fuel costs
item.description: The Irish military will be deployed to move vehicles "blocking critical infrastructure".

item.title: The Hit Erotica Writers Outwitting Nigeria’s Religious Censors
item.description: Zealous officials burned their predecessors’ romance novels. Now, young Muslim women in northern Nigeria publish their erotic books in installments on WhatsApp.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Israel Strikes Hezbollah as Lebanon Impasse Threatens Cease-Fire
item.description: Israel said it would continue striking the Iran-backed militia. Iran said it would not attend peace talks with the United States in Pakistan if the truce was not extended to Lebanon.

item.title: Islamabad, Pakistan’s quiet capital, steps into the diplomatic spotlight.
Xi Meets Taiwan Opposition Leader for First Time in a Decade
item.description: Xi Jinping held rare talks with a Taiwanese opposition leader, Cheng Li-wun, who floated the idea of some day inviting Mr. Xi to visit Taiwan.

item.title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Only a few vessels have crossed the strait since the US-Iran ceasefire deal, according to BBC Verify analysis.

item.title: Scam Inc has a new weapon
item.description: For the first time, investigators have traced spyware to its physical origin—revealing new types of duplicity

item.title: SNP pledges 'first refusal' for tenants to buy private rental homes
item.description: John Swinney says it would help renters get on the housing ladder but other parties say the SNP is to blame for the housing emergency.

item.title: With Iran Setting Limits, Strait of Hormuz Remains Thorny Politically
item.description: Even after a cease-fire, Iran is keeping a chokehold on traffic, forcing countries to cut deals that could put them at odds with the U.S.

item.title: German transgender far-right extremist arrested in Czech Republic
item.description: German media reports say Marla-Svenja Liebich used to be a member of a neo-Nazi group called Blood and Honour.

item.title: What to Know About the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
item.description: The truce is under strain because of two issues, Lebanon and the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. and Iranian negotiators are scheduled to meet in Pakistan this weekend.

item.title: European airports face jet fuel shortages within three weeks
item.description: Industry group warns EU that reserves are running low

item.title: FirstFT: Iran war to ‘scar’ markets, Wall Street warns
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: US-Iran talks in Pakistan, and financial figures discuss Anthropic’s AI model threat

item.title: Lebanon: Images of a Nation Uprooted by War
item.description: A million people in Lebanon have fled their homes as Israel clashes anew with Hezbollah. This is what their lives look now.

item.title: See You in Pyongyang: Russia Pushes Its People to Embrace North Korea
item.description: Through art, food, tourism and academics, an increasingly isolated Moscow is seeking to cement lasting ties with Pyongyang.

item.title: Vance Faces Test of His Negotiating Skills With Iran Talks
item.description: Vice President JD Vance is leading negotiations this weekend toward an end to a war that he had opposed starting.

item.title: This Hungarian Town Explains Why Orban Could Lose on Sunday
item.description: Lake Balaton was beloved as a vacation spot. Now, luxury real estate projects serve many friends of Prime Minister Viktor Orban, and the locals are fed up.

item.title: Soaring Fuel Prices Squeeze China’s Frugal Truckers
item.description: Many long-haul truckers in China have a tight budget and live frugally on the road, sleeping and cooking in their vehicles. Now, the pain at the pump is forcing some drivers to rethink their lives.

item.title: Many Venezuelans Want to Go Home. But They Can’t Get Passports.
item.description: The U.S. has made it impossible for them to stay legally, and their own country makes it difficult for them to return.

item.title: Israel presses ahead with Lebanon strikes as talks loom
item.description: Iran’s attendance at peace discussions in Islamabad on Saturday uncertain amid strained truce

item.title: The NHS isn’t getting better
item.description: Tough medicine is needed to resolve rolling strikes

item.title: Jollof, jewellery and Ridley Road Market – Simone Brewster shares her London
item.description: The artist and designer on the African and Caribbean destinations that make her city home

item.title: Architect Sophie Hicks: my love affair with Seoul
item.description: A decade of projects in the South Korean capital have made the designer fall for its youthful buzz

item.title: US has let in 4,499 refugees since October - all but three were South African
item.description: Trump has given priority to Afrikaners, a white minority group he says is being persecuted. South Africa has objected to his characterisation.

item.title: UK elections poll tracker
item.description: Live-updating snapshot of party and leadership support in run-up to voting on May 7

item.title: Taiwan opposition leader meets Xi Jinping in Beijing
item.description: Cheng Li-wun is the Kuomintang's first sitting leader to visit China in a decade.

item.title: Middle East War Triggers Higher Prices in China
item.description: Three and a half years of deflationary pressure on Chinese factories reversed course last month as higher energy prices cycled into the economy.

item.title: Submit your questions: How will the Iran war impact China?
item.description: Take part in a live Ask an Expert Q&A with Joe Leahy and Cheng Leng from our Beijing bureau today at 1pm BST

item.title: 'We should not be at the mercy of events abroad' - Starmer
item.description: The prime minister says "shocks" like the conflict in Iran are becoming more frequent.

item.title: Ukrainian interceptors downed Iran’s drones in Middle East, says Zelenskyy
item.description: Ukraine’s president says military teams dispatched across the Gulf had delivered ‘positive’ results

item.title: China’s Xi tells Taiwan opposition leader unification an ‘inevitability’
item.description: Meeting in Beijing with Chinese president first of its kind in a decade

item.title: Europe braces for Hungary’s pivotal election as Orbán fights for survival
item.description: Plus: a €2.2bn Chinese deal tests Brussels approach to economic security

item.title: North Sea oil prices hit record high as Iran keeps hold over Hormuz
item.description: Washington-Tehran ceasefire agreement fails to stem global energy crunch

item.title: China’s factory gate prices rise on back of Iran war
item.description: Producer price index for the world’s dominant manufacturer turns positive year-on-year for the first time since 2022

item.title: South African Writer Mfundi Vundla Gets Personal in His New Play
item.description: Mfundi Vundla spent 21 years in exile and created the popular television show “Generations.” His latest project is a play that explores the imperfections of the fight against apartheid.

item.title: Labour’s deputy warns against ‘bloody’ leadership contest to replace Starmer
item.description: Lucy Powell, an ally of Andy Burnham, says party members would take ‘dim view’ of challenge despite looming election losses

item.title: UK health officials discuss banning doctors from going on strike
item.description: Wes Streeting has not ruled out policy as desperation grows over walkouts in long-running pay dispute

item.title: Annuities look more attractive in wake of inflation fears, say advisers
item.description: Benefits of a fixed and stable income set to play more prominent role in retirement planning

item.title: EU boosts imports of Russian gas as Middle East crisis squeezes supplies
item.description: The bloc took 97% of the cargoes from the vast Yamal LNG project in Siberia in the first three months of year

item.title: Now would be a great time to send US ambassadors to the Middle East
item.description: At a time when many question American leadership, there’s an underrated value in simply showing up

item.title: Nato split over US access to European bases in Iran war
item.description: Donald Trump irate at France and Spain’s refusal to authorise use of military facilities

item.title: Iran war will leave a long-term ‘scar’ on Wall Street, investors warn
item.description: Commodity prices and bond yields unlikely to return to pre-conflict levels quickly

item.title: Trump says Iran's handling of Strait of Hormuz is 'not the agreement we have'
item.description: The US president accused Iran of "doing a very poor job" with the waterway as Israel and Lebanon to begin peace talks.

item.title: India proposes new rules to regulate news and political posts on social media
item.description: The changes will give government more power over news-related posts shared by podcasters and influencers.

item.title: Iran’s Battered Leaders Emerge From War Confident — and With New Cards
item.description: For Iran’s theocratic rulers, just surviving the U.S.-Israeli onslaught means victory. But the seeds of their next crisis may already be planted.

item.title: Co-leader McIlroy's golf does the talking after busy Masters build-up
item.description: With the pomp and celebration of his Masters victory finished, Rory McIlroy reminded everyone he is back at Augusta to win again.

item.title: How the cost of holidays, food and clothes is rising - despite the Iran ceasefire
item.description: Fuel costs have already soared, flights and mortgages are also rising - and food could soon do the same.

item.title: The Costs of Trump’s Iran-War Folly
item.description: If this is “total and complete victory,” imagine what failure looks like.

item.title: EU fingerprint and photo travel rules come into force
item.description: The EU's much-delayed Entry/Exit System will change the way UK passengers travel to 29 countries.

item.title: Spreading Islamist insurgency dominates Benin's presidential campaign
item.description: Cross-border raids from militant Islamists are increasingly threatening the West African state's stability.

item.title: Ceasefire or no ceasefire, the Middle East's reshuffling is not yet done
item.description: Both sides have reason to end the war but share no common ground.

item.title: 3 Russian Submarines Detected Near Britain Were Spying, U.K. Says
item.description: John Healey, the defense secretary, said the vessels were gathering information about undersea pipelines, and said he believed President Vladimir V. Putin “would want us to be distracted by the Middle East.”

item.title: Russia and Ukraine agree to truce for Orthodox Easter
item.description: Vladimir Putin announced the truce, which will last from Saturday afternoon on 11 April through Easter Sunday.

item.title: Hot in the city: Energy crisis tests Singapore's air-con addiction
item.description: The rise in energy prices has hit Asia particularly hard as many nations are heavily reliant on Gulf oil.

item.title: Zelensky Sees Small Window for Peace
item.description: Talks to end the war in Ukraine could resume soon, said President Volodymyr Zelensky as he expressed skepticism about a breakthrough.

item.title: A Newspaper Is Raided and a Rights Group Outlawed Amid Kremlin Crackdown
item.description: As Moscow throttles Russia’s few remaining independent voices, the authorities targeted two of the most prominent, one a Nobel Peace Prize winner and the other headed by a Nobel laureate.

item.title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
item.description: The project was part of a package of tech investment promising the UK could become an AI superpower.

item.title: 137-Year-Old Piece of Eiffel Tower to Be Auctioned in Paris
item.description: A section of the landmark’s original staircase will go up for auction next month and could sell for a towering sum.

item.title: Lebanon thought there was a ceasefire - then Israel unleashed deadly blitz
item.description: Israel says Lebanon is not included in the ceasefire the US agreed to halt the war with Iran.

item.title: Israeli strikes in Lebanon 'grave violation' of ceasefire, Iran minister tells BBC
item.description: Iran's deputy foreign minister says the US must choose between 'war and ceasefire'.

item.title: UK says Russia ran submarine operation over cables and pipelines
item.description: Defence Secretary John Healey says there is "no evidence" of any damage to UK infrastructure in the Atlantic.

item.title: Greens launch local election campaign with focus on housing
item.description: Party leader Zack Polanski accuses Labour of failing to build enough social and affordable homes.

item.title: 'Endless fears': Even if fighting stops, the damage to Iran's children will endure
item.description: The BBC has been able to obtain testimony from parents and those trying to help children deal with the distress that comes with war.

item.title: French far-right leader romantically linked to Italian princess
item.description: Presidential hopeful Jordan Bardella was pictured on a Corsican beach with Princess Maria Carolina of Bourbon-Two Sicilies.

item.title: Payments for children and 100 new GPs in Plaid election manifesto
item.description: Rhun ap Iorwerth said a Plaid government would cut waiting times, help with childcare costs and grow economy.

item.title: US warns citizens to reconsider Nigeria travel on safety grounds
item.description: The state department has also authorised non-emergency embassy staff to leave on security grounds.

item.title: BTS battle torrential rain to kick off $1bn world tour
item.description: The K-Pop band are battered by the elements as they launch their marathon world tour in Goyang.

item.title: Four people die trying to board boat in Channel crossing attempt
item.description: Authorities say 37 people are being treated in hospital, but around 30 others continued their journeys to the UK.

item.title: Has US achieved its war objectives in Iran?
item.description: Key US objectives at the start of the war were to stop Iran getting a nuclear weapon and degrading its arsenal.

item.title: Why a big country like Italy acts as if it were small
item.description: Equivocating between Trump and Europe suits a history of switching sides

item.title: Viktor Orban is bashing Ukraine for votes
item.description: Hungary’s election poisons relations between Hungary and its neighbour

item.title: Europe’s joint nuclear-fusion project needs Russian expertise
item.description: At ITER Russian and Western scientists still work together—slowly

item.title: Most Syrians in Germany are there to stay
item.description: Sending back masses of immigrants would be foolish and impossible

item.title: A deadly conflict in Malawi raises questions about conservation
item.description: How can humans coexist with wild animals?

item.title: Burkina Faso’s government is committing war crimes
item.description: While fighting jihadism, it has killed more civilians than the jihadists themselves

item.title: How anarchist was Africa?
item.description: Precolonial statelessness may have been a sign of success, not failure

item.title: Africa’s protests span countries, classes and causes
item.description: But can they bring lasting change?

item.title: Cambodia honours a life-saving rat
item.description: The rodent helped clear more than 100 unexploded mines

item.title: China may be building a big new airbase in the South China Sea
item.description: But analysts are scratching their heads over the timing and location

item.title: There is little prospect of legalising abortion in Brazil
item.description: Other large countries in Latin America have either legalised or decriminalised

item.title: The West is doing more to combat China’s covert activity abroad
item.description: But China is paying little attention

item.title: Sir Keir Starmer is Britain’s best hope for legalising assisted dying
item.description: He should stop dawdling

item.title: Recriminations over Iran have heightened the risk of a break-up of NATO
item.description: Europe must prepare to defend itself

item.title: Caf president would welcome corruption investigation
item.description: Confederation of African Football president Patrice Motsepe has "zero tolerance" for corruption, which he says is "worse than cancer".

item.title: Kenya disputes UN report on rape allegations against its Haiti personnel
item.description: The foreign minister says the accusations, involving children, were investigated and found to be unsubstantiated.

item.title: Al Jazeera condemns killing of journalist in Israeli strike in Gaza
item.description: The Israeli military claims Mohammed Wishah was a "Hamas terrorist" - which the Qatar-based network has previously denied.

item.title: Husband arrested over disappearance of Michigan woman in Bahamas
item.description: The lawyer of Brian Hooker, who was identified as the arrested man by US media, says he denies any wrongdoing.

item.title: India’s religious minorities face harsher anti-conversion laws
item.description: Exhumations fuel communal tensions

item.title: How Pakistan emerged as an unlikely broker of peace in the Gulf
item.description: The troubled country has cannily managed its ties with the warring parties

item.title: Argentina passes bill loosening protection of its glaciers
item.description: The new bill will make it easier for provincial governments to allow mining projects in glacier regions.

item.title: Key Indian state polls begin in test for Modi's party
item.description: Modi's Bharatiya Janata Party is trying to expand into regions where it has struggled to gain a foothold.

item.title: Trapped miner rescued from flooded Mexican tunnel after 14 days
item.description: The 42-year-old gold miner was standing in waist-high water when rescuers found him in a flooded tunnel.

item.title: Donald Trump is the war’s biggest loser
item.description: There is a reason he wants an exit from Iran

item.title: Conservatives vow to outlaw council four-day week
item.description: South Cambridgeshire District Council is the only authority to offer four-day weeks for full pay.

item.title: A ceasefire will not prevent the Iran war’s economic harm
item.description: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens, expect lasting change to energy markets

item.title: He's Australia's most decorated soldier. Now he's at the centre of a historic war crimes case
item.description: Ben Roberts-Smith's case is not only unprecedented for Australia but "extraordinary" for the globe too, scholars say.

item.title: BBC at the site of Israeli air strikes in Beirut
item.description: At least 182 people were killed and 890 others wounded, according to the Lebanese health ministry.

item.title: A Cease-Fire for Now in Iran, but a Blow to American Credibility
item.description: Critics wonder if this is America’s “Suez moment,” when a leading power signals the start of its international decline.

item.title: Is BTS losing its K-pop identity as it aims for a bigger world stage?
item.description: BTS turned millions on to K-pop. But now it’s caught between Korea and the world.

item.title: Watch: Pope Leo XIV spins basketball on his finger, with help from Harlem Globetrotters
item.description: US exhibition squad, the Harlem Globetrotters, met the pontiff in the Vatican, helping him to spin a basketball on the tip of his finger.

item.title: Chinese researcher's death after questioning in US prompts anger in Beijing
item.description: The semiconductor researcher was found dead after “hostile questioning” by US law enforcement.

item.title: Starmer says much work remains to make US-Iran ceasefire hold
item.description: The prime minister says fully reopening the Strait of Hormuz would help "stabilise" prices in the UK.

item.title: NHS questions dominate election leaders' programme
item.description: The Conservatives, Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats faced audience questions in Haverfordwest.

item.title: Israel backs US-Iran ceasefire but Netanyahu's war goals remain unfulfilled
item.description: The Israeli prime minister's response to the ceasefire has been muted, in contrast to the triumphal statements from the US and Iran.

item.title: At least 182 killed across Lebanon in large wave of Israeli strikes
item.description: Attacks hit the southern suburbs of Beirut, southern Lebanon and the eastern Bekaa Valley, hours after a US-Iran ceasefire was announced.

item.title: Negotiators face huge task to close gaps in rival Iran peace proposals
item.description: The US has a 15-point plan to end the war, with Iran offering 10 proposals of its own. Can they be reconciled?

item.title: Gambia appoints British barrister to prosecute gruesome Jammeh-era crimes
item.description: Ex-President Yahya Jammeh's 22-year rule was known for enforced disappearances and extrajudicial killings.

item.title: Who is Viktor Orbán, Hungarian PM fighting to stay in power after 16 years?
item.description: Since 2010, Orbán has transformed Hungary into what the European Parliament has denounced as a "hybrid regime of electoral autocracy".

item.title: Partner, scapegoat or spoiler? Israel’s place in a fragile ceasefire
item.description: The dealmaking between America and Iran is happening without Binyamin Netanyahu

item.title: Why ceasefire deal with US has unsettled Iran's hardliners
item.description: The two-week truce opens up the prospect of direct talks with the US but has angered Iran's hardliners, writes BBC News Persian’s Kasra Naji.

item.title: Watch: Fire damages roof of Rio's Olympic velodrome
item.description: Around 80 firefighters and 20 fire trucks tackled the blaze, the state's military fire department said, adding that no one was hurt.

item.title: Bowen: Ceasefire means respite for civilians, but it might not last long
item.description: Whether or not a lasting peace deal can be reached, the war and its consequences are reshaping the Middle East, writes international editor Jeremy Bowen.

item.title: Government approves UK's largest solar farm
item.description: The Springwell solar farm in North Kesteven would cover an area the size of 1,700 football pitches.

item.title: How France learned to fight Russian disinformation
item.description: The EU’s most-targeted country is counterattacking against fake news

item.title: Germany suspends military approval for long stays abroad for men under 45
item.description: The requirement to obtain permission had gone largely unnoticed until a German newspaper reported on it.

item.title: Nigeria begins mass trial of 500 terrorism suspects
item.description: Very few people are ever prosecuted following terror attacks with suspects often held without trial.

item.title: Lib Dems pledge extra £300m for social care at Senedd campaign launch
item.description: Leader Jane Dodds says her party can "influence the direction of the next government" after 7 May.

item.title: Cameroon 'military contractors' killed in Russia-Ukraine war - BBC confirms leaked message
item.description: The government usually refrains from commenting on the participation of its citizens in the conflict.

item.title: Greece to ban social media for under-15s from next year
item.description: It follows similar moves in other European countries, including France and Spain.

item.title: British teen dies in reported Vietnam motorbike fall
item.description: Orla Wates is remembered by her mother as "beautiful, independent and very funny".

item.title: Man fined after breaking into viral hippo Moo Deng's enclosure
item.description: The director of the Thai zoo where Moo Deng lives said security would be upped after the incident.

item.title: Co-founder of Jalisco New Generation drug cartel pleads guilty
item.description: Érick Valencia Salazar was among a group of 29 alleged drug lords extradited to the US from Mexico.

item.title: Hospitals coping well with doctors' strike so far - NHS boss
item.description: Resident doctors in England – the new name for junior doctors – are taking part in their 15th walkout in a long-running pay dispute.

item.title: How Pakistan helped secure a fragile ceasefire between the US and Iran
item.description: Pakistan has a historic relationship with Iran, but an agreement was far from certain, writes the BBC’s Caroline Davies

item.title: Iran and America agree to pause their war
item.description: A two-week truce is a start but questions remain about a more enduring deal

item.title: Iran ceasefire deal gives Trump a way out of war - but at a high cost
item.description: The path to the two-week ceasefire with Iran may have fundamentally altered the way the rest of the world views the US.

item.title: Plan to scrap presidential elections puts Zimbabweans at loggerheads
item.description: The ruling party has unveiled a draft law that strips voters of the chance to elect the president.

item.title: The US refinery now processing Venezuelan oil
item.description: Chevron is now importing 250,000 barrels of crude per day from Venezuela.

item.title: Honduran newlywed wife of US soldier freed by ICE after detention
item.description: Annie Ramos, an undocumented immigrant who came to the US as a toddler, had gone to the base to complete paperwork so the couple could move in together.

item.title: Artemis II has offered Earth inspiration
item.description: But rekindled Space Age enthusiasm will need new foundations if it is to endure

item.title: Wireless Festival cancelled after Kanye West blocked from coming to UK
item.description: West was due to headline the festival in July but drew criticism over past antisemitic comments.

item.title: JD Vance backs Orbán's re-election bid in Budapest visit and hits out at EU
item.description: US Vice-President JD Vance intervenes in the campaign to give Orbán a ringing endorsement in Budapest.

item.title: Fifa starts disciplinary action over Spain v Egypt chants
item.description: Fifa opens disciplinary proceedings against the Spanish FA after "Islamophobic and xenophobic" chants during a friendly between Spain and Egypt last month.

item.title: Did Israel overestimate the damage to Iran’s missile programme?
item.description: Iran shows no sign of running out

item.title: Schools in knife crime hotspots to get specialist training
item.description: A new partnership will provide training for school leaders about knife-crime risk, the government says.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: A terrifying wait
item.description: Gregg Carlstrom, our Middle East correspondent, on Donald Trump’s blood-curdling threat to erase Iran

item.title: A wary rapprochement between India and China
item.description: New Indian investment rules are a cautious start

item.title: Scottish Conservatives pledge tax cuts in election manifesto
item.description: Leader Russell Findlay announces his party's plans ahead of next month's Holyrood poll.

item.title: Carnival fever hits Lagos as locals celebrate Afro-Brazilian heritage
item.description: The centuries-old Fanti Carnival returned to Nigeria on Monday with a burst of colour and spectacle.

item.title: Reform would deny visas over calls for slavery reparations
item.description: The party says it wants to put African and Caribbean countries "on notice" after a vote at the UN last month.

item.title: One dead as train travelling 99mph collides with lorry in France
item.description: The lorry driver is in custody after the train driver died during the collision in northern France.

item.title: Greens promise rent controls and £1 bus fares in Welsh election manifesto
item.description: The Greens' set of promises for the Senedd election also include free bus travel for under-22s.

item.title: Chilean woman accused of Pinochet-era kidnaps loses extradition battle
item.description: Adriana Rivas moved from Chile to Australia in the 1970s, working there as a nanny and cleaner.

item.title: Plan 2 student loan interest rates capped at 6% in England
item.description: The cap on Plan 2 and postgraduate loan interest rates comes amid a risk of rising inflation.
